facts.habitat: Lakes, rivers, parks, golf courses, and suburban ponds
facts.diet: Grasses, grains, aquatic vegetation, and berries
facts.migration: Partially migratory; northern populations move south in autumn

hints.region: Found across North America — from Arctic Canada to the Gulf Coast; populations have expanded greatly in urban areas
hints.size: A large goose — up to 110 cm and weighing up to 6.5 kg, with a wingspan nearly as wide as a person is tall
hints.behavior: Grazes in large flocks on lawns and open areas; known for its honking call and for aggressively defending nests near water

funFact: Canada Geese fly in a V-formation during migration because each bird benefits from the uplift created by the wingtip vortex of the bird ahead.

facts.habitat: Forests, deserts, swamps, and cities — one of the most adaptable owls
facts.diet: Rabbits, hares, squirrels, mice, other birds (including smaller owls), and skunks

hints.region: Found across virtually all of North and South America — one of the most widespread owls on Earth
hints.size: Large and powerful — up to 63 cm tall with a wingspan exceeding 1.4 metres and the strongest grip of any North American owl
hints.behavior: Begins nesting in mid-winter while snow is still on the ground; its deep hoot-hoot call is one of the most recognizable night sounds in North America

funFact: Great Horned Owls have no true horns — the 'ear tufts' are just feathers used for camouflage and communication.

facts.habitat: Open areas with scattered trees — fields, roadsides, deserts, and forest edges
facts.diet: Small mammals, especially rodents; also rabbits, snakes, and birds
facts.migration: Partially migratory; many populations resident year-round

hints.region: The most common hawk in North America — seen year-round from Alaska to Panama
hints.size: A large, stocky raptor — about 50 cm with a wingspan of over a metre; often seen circling high overhead on thermal updrafts
hints.behavior: Frequently perches on roadside poles and fence posts watching for prey; the piercing cry used as the 'generic hawk scream' in movies and TV

funFact: The distinctive red tail of adult Red-tailed Hawks appears only after their second year of life — juveniles have a brown-banded tail instead.

facts.habitat: Lakes, rivers, ponds, wetlands, and urban park ponds
facts.diet: Aquatic vegetation, seeds, insects, worms, and small fish
facts.migration: Partially migratory; northern populations move south in winter

hints.region: Found across the northern hemisphere — one of the most abundant and widespread ducks in the world
hints.size: A typical 'duck-sized duck' — about 55–60 cm from bill to tail, stocky and low in the water
hints.behavior: Dabbles for food at the water's surface rather than diving; the familiar 'quack' is made by the female — males give a quieter, raspier call

funFact: Most domestic duck breeds worldwide are descended from the wild Mallard, domesticated thousands of years ago in China.

facts.habitat: Open areas with weedy fields, roadsides, gardens, and woodland edges
facts.diet: Almost exclusively seeds, especially thistles and sunflowers
facts.migration: Short-distance migrant; moves south within North America in winter

hints.region: Found across North America — common at garden feeders in summer across the US and southern Canada
hints.size: Tiny — about 12 cm and weighing only 11–20 grams, roughly the weight of three quarters
hints.behavior: One of the strictest vegetarians among birds; feeds almost exclusively on seeds and even feeds its chicks regurgitated seeds rather than insects

funFact: The American Goldfinch is one of the latest breeders in North America, waiting until thistles and other seed plants are in full seed in mid-summer.
